notes: Official Origin product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Looking at your transaction history, you're running almost everything through your Chase Sapphire Preferred 8600 — which is actually a solid foundation, but there's meaningful reward optimization available depending on how you're using it.
Your current spending picture
Based on last month's transactions, here's where your dollars are going:
Groceries: $671.85 (Costco Wholesale, Trader Joe's, Safeway) — your single largest variable category
Travel & vacation: $560.64 (Alaska Airlines, Avis Rent A Car) — strong category spend
Drinks & dining: $162.11 (Tavolata, Ramen Danbo, Oddfellows Cafe)
Subscriptions: $82.98 (Adobe $59.99, Netflix $22.99)
Personal care: $89.00 (ClassPass)
Transportation: $61.90 (Uber, Lyft)
Phone: $72.00 (AT&T Wireless)
Utilities: $89.00 (Xfinity)
Auto & transport: $58.23 (Costco Gas)
How your Chase Sapphire Preferred performs today
The Sapphire Preferred earns 3x on dining and 2x on travel, which means your restaurant and airline spending is already well-optimized. Where you're likely leaving rewards on the table is in the other categories — groceries, subscriptions, gas, utilities, and phone typically earn just 1x on most travel cards, which is the baseline "floor" rate.
Where you could earn more
Groceries ($672/month — your biggest opportunity)
This is your highest-impact category. A dedicated grocery card earning 5-6% back here could generate roughly $40/month in rewards — about $480/year — compared to perhaps $7/month at 1x. The gap is significant given your Costco, Trader Joe's, and Safeway spread. Worth noting: Costco purchases sometimes require a Visa card specifically, so card network matters here.
Gas (Costco Gas ~$58/month)
Costco Gas is similarly strong territory for a dedicated 3-5% gas card. At 1x you're earning pennies; at 3% you'd capture an extra $1.75/month — modest, but if paired with a grocery card it adds up.
Subscriptions (~$83/month)
Adobe and Netflix would do well on a card with fixed bonus categories for streaming and software. Some cards earn 5% on rotating categories that include streaming services, or 3% fixed.
Transportation (Uber/Lyft ~$62/month)
Some cards — including certain travel cards — offer 3x on rideshare specifically. Worth checking whether your Sapphire Preferred counts these as travel (it may, which would make this already 2x).
Phone & utilities (~$161/month combined)
These are typically "dead zones" on most travel cards. A flat 2% cash-back card as a catch-all would immediately double your effective return on AT&T, Xfinity, and ClassPass compared to 1x.
A practical pairing strategy
The highest-yield everyday setup — consistent with what current market data suggests for 2026 — is typically:
Keep the Sapphire Preferred for dining, travel bookings, and airline charges (your Alaska Airlines and Avis spend is already perfectly placed here)
Add a dedicated grocery/gas card earning 5-6% to capture your $670+ monthly grocery spend
Add a flat 2% catch-all card for everything else — subscriptions, utilities, phone, ClassPass — so you're never earning just 1% on uncategorized spend
The key mental rule: use the highest-rate card for each category until any caps are hit, then fall back to the 2% card.
One important note on your accounts
I notice your accounts are connected manually with $0 balances showing — it may be worth ensuring your Robinhood brokerage and 401(k) balances are fully updated so we can factor your complete financial picture into any rewards strategy decisions.
Would you like help mapping each of your specific recurring vendors to the optimal card in a potential two- or three-card setup?

Results aren't guaranteed. See disclosures.

Jan
